disease.name: Interstitial Lung Disease / Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis
disease.note: ILD encompasses a diverse group of diffuse parenchymal lung diseases. IPF is the most common idiopathic interstitial pneumonia, characterised by the UIP pattern on HRCT. ATS/ERS criteria guide diagnosis. Antifibrotic therapy with pirfenidone or nintedanib slows progression.

immediateDangerSigns.description: Acute respiratory worsening with new bilateral GGO on HRCT in <1 month

historyFeatures.note: Dry, non-productive cough is hallmark; often paroxysmal and refractory to antitussives
May indicate connective tissue disease-associated ILD
GERD is common in IPF; chronic microaspiration may drive fibrosis
Asbestos, silica, coal, metal dusts, bird antigens (HP)
Rare in IPF; suggests alternative (vasculitis, malignancy, PE)

examFeatures.note: Fine, velcro-like inspiratory crackles at bases; hallmark of IPF
Up to 50% of IPF; uncommon in sarcoidosis

riskFactors.note: IPF is associated with cigarette smoking
Asbestos, silica, coal, metal dusts, wood dust, bird antigens
Familial IPF accounts for 5–20% of cases
Amiodarone, methotrexate, bleomycin, nitrofurantoin, checkpoint inhibitors
TERT, TERC, PARN, SFTPC, SFTPA2 mutations

subtypes.name: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF)
subtypes.management: Antifibrotic therapy (pirfenidone or nintedanib); lung transplant evaluation; oxygen; pulmonary rehabilitation

subtypes.name: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (HP)
subtypes.management: Antigen avoidance; corticosteroids; antifibrotics if fibrotic HP

subtypes.name: CTD-associated ILD
subtypes.management: Treat underlying CTD; immunosuppression; antifibrotics if progressive fibrosis

subtypes.name: Pulmonary Sarcoidosis
subtypes.management: Observation if asymptomatic; corticosteroids if progressive; steroid-sparing agents (methotrexate, azathioprine)

bedside.name: Pulse oximetry with desaturation walk test
bedside.indication: all suspected ILD; desaturation ≥4% is significant

laboratory.name: Serology for CTD (ANA, RF, anti-CCP, anti-Scl-70, anti-Jo1, anti-Ro, anti-La)
laboratory.note: ATS/ERS recommends serology in all ILD patients

laboratory.name: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis panel (avian/thermophilic antigens)
laboratory.indication: suspected HP based on exposure history

laboratory.name: HRCT chest (high-resolution)
laboratory.indication: essential for diagnosis and pattern recognition

imaging.name: HRCT chest (supine + prone)
imaging.indication: all patients; UIP pattern (basal+subpleural honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, reticulation)

typicalFindings.UIP: basal predominant, subpleural, honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, reticulation
typicalFindings.NSIP: bibasal GGO, fine reticulation, relative subpleural sparing
typicalFindings.HP: centrilobular nodules, mosaic attenuation, air trapping

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.indication: initial chest imaging; reticulation, volume loss

advanced.name: Pulmonary function tests (spirometry, lung volumes, DLCO)
advanced.note: Restrictive pattern (reduced TLC, FVC), reduced DLCO. FVC decline >10% in 6 months is poor prognosis

advanced.name: Bronchoscopy with BAL
advanced.indication: exclude infection, HP (lymphocytosis), malignancy, alveolar proteinosis
advanced.note: BAL lymphocytosis >30% suggests HP or sarcoidosis; neutrophils and eosinophils in IPF

advanced.name: Surgical lung biopsy (cryobiopsy or VATS)
advanced.indication: when HRCT pattern is non-diagnostic and therapy decisions depend on diagnosis
advanced.note: ATS/ERS recommends multidisciplinary discussion (MDD) before biopsy

management.protocolReference: ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT IPF Guidelines 2022; ATS/ERS ILD Classification 2024

pathways.criteria: FVC >70%, DLCO >50%, minimal symptoms, limited HRCT extent
treatment: Monitor every 3–6 months with PFTs and 6MWT
GERD treatment if present (PPI or H2 antagonist)
Avoid drugs known to cause ILD

pathways.criteria: FVC 50–70%, DLCO 30–50%, symptomatic, HRCT shows fibrosis
treatment: Antifibrotic therapy: pirfenidone 2403 mg/day (3× 801 mg) or nintedanib 300 mg/day (2× 150 mg)
Pirfenidone reduces FVC decline by ~50% over 1 year; nintedanib reduces decline by ~44%
Monitor for side effects: GI intolerance, photosensitivity (pirfenidone); diarrhoea, LFT elevation (nintedanib)
Evaluate for lung transplant referral (early referral recommended per ATS/ERS)

pathways.criteria: FVC <50% or DLCO <30%, significant hypoxia, advanced disease on HRCT
treatment: Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) if PaO2 <7.3 kPa or exertional desaturation
Palliative care integration: symptom management (cough, dyspnea, anxiety)
Lung transplant assessment and listing (survival benefit highest in IPF; median survival without transplant 3–5 years)
Acute exacerbation management: high-dose corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1 g IV × 3 days), empiric antibiotics, supportive care
Manage pulmonary hypertension if present (consider sildenafil as add-on)
pathways.escalation: Acute exacerbation of IPF is a medical emergency with high mortality. Consider ICU if potentially reversible.

complications.management: High-dose corticosteroids, empiric antibiotics, supportive care; mortality 50%

complications.note: Increased risk of lung cancer in IPF, especially squamous cell

disease.emergencyPriority: if acute exacerbation (sudden worsening, new bilateral GGO) or severe hypoxia
diagnosticClues: progressive exertional dyspnoea with dry cough
UIP pattern on HRCT: subpleural basal honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis
HRCT sensitivity for UIP is >90% when present
exclusionClues: obstructive spirometry with hyperinflation suggests COPD/emphysema
normal HRCT effectively excludes fibrotic ILD
improvement with antigen avoidance suggests HP
significant pleural disease suggests asbestos-related or CTD
crackles that clear after cough suggest bronchitis/bronchiectasis
